summary: My family's roots trace back to Andalusia, specifically a small village in Granada called Cuevas del Campo. Andalusia was where my ancestors lived before my father emigrated in the 1960s seeking work in Barcelona. It holds a special place in my heart as the origin of our family history and culture.

Granada is the province in Andalusia where my family's village, Cuevas del Campo, is located. It is the place where my parents come from and where I have traveled every summer since I was a baby. Granada represents the connection to my heritage and the rural life my family left behind to seek better opportunities.

Cuevas del Campo is the small village in Granada where my family originally lived. Since I was 10 months old, I have traveled there every summer from Hospitalet, first by Paquillo bus and later by my father's SEAT 650 car. It is a place full of family memories and tradition, and I continue to visit it yearly with my own family.

Spain is my home country, where my family lived before moving to Barcelona in the 1960s. The industrialization of northern Spain, especially Catalonia, drew my parents there for work and better opportunities. Spain remains central to my identity, and I maintain strong ties through yearly visits to my family's village.

Barcelona was the city my father moved to in the 1960s looking for work during Catalonia's industrial boom. My mother joined him two years later, staying with my uncle Fernando and Maria Josefa in an apartment built by Spanish Construccions. Barcelona was where my parents started their new life and where my family grew before moving to Hospitalet.

Catalonia is the region in northern Spain that industrialized rapidly in the 1960s, attracting my father to move there for work. It includes Barcelona and surrounding areas where my family settled. Catalonia offered better jobs and educational opportunities, which is why many families like mine migrated there from southern Spain.

Spanish Construccions was the company that built the apartment where my uncle Fernando and Maria Josefa lived in Barcelona. My mother stayed there when she first arrived in the city. This organization indirectly supported my family's early years in Barcelona by providing housing for immigrants like us.

La Verneda was the neighborhood in Barcelona where my family first lived after arriving from Andalusia. My mother's sister Ceferina also lived there, and my parents often went there on weekends to meet family and friends. It was a place of community and connection for us during those early years in Catalonia.

Hospitalet de Llobregat is where my brother Ricardo and I were born after my family moved from La Verneda. We lived on Alpes Street in a rented apartment, and it became our home base before I eventually moved to the United States. Hospitalet represents my childhood and the continuation of my family's life in Catalonia.

El Prat de Llobregat is where my mother and I used to go to bring wool and knit. It was part of our family routine alongside sewing and other household tasks. This place is connected to the daily life and traditions my mother maintained while raising us in Catalonia.

Paquillo was the bus company that transported emigrants like my family from Barcelona to our village in Cuevas del Campo every summer. The trip took between 12 and 15 hours, stopping in many villages along the way. This service was essential for maintaining our connection to our roots and visiting family back home.

The SEAT 650 was my father's car that we used to travel from Hospitalet to Cuevas del Campo after initially taking the Paquillo bus. It became a symbol of our family's journeys and the tradition of returning to our village every summer. I have fond memories of those long drives in the SEAT 650 with my family.

In 1991, I participated in an exchange program called Amity Aid for teachers in Minnesota, United States. This program was a turning point that led me to live in Saint Paul, Minnesota, where I have been since. Amity Aid opened new opportunities for me beyond Spain and connected me to a new life abroad.

Minnesota is the state in the United States where I moved in 1991 through the Amity Aid exchange program. I settled in Saint Paul and have lived there ever since. Minnesota became my new home, where my mother later joined me, and where I raised my son Pedro.

The United States became my home starting in 1991 when I moved there for a teaching exchange program. Living in Saint Paul, Minnesota, I built a new life while maintaining strong ties to Spain through yearly visits. The US represents both opportunity and a bridge between my past and present.

Saint Paul, Minnesota is where I have lived since 1991 after moving there through the Amity Aid program. My mother Sabina joined me there in 2002 when my son Pedro was born, and we have made it our family home. Despite living in Saint Paul, we continue the tradition of traveling back to Cuevas del Campo every year.
